title: Lemon drizzle cake
subtitle: A sharp, syrup-soaked loaf cake with a crunchy lemon sugar crust.
excerpt: A moist loaf sponge made with lemon zest, pierced while still warm and drenched in lemon juice mixed with sugar so the syrup soaks in and forms a crisp, sugary crust on top. One of the most popular British home bakes. Serves eight.
sourceNotes: Lemon drizzle cake. The lemon drizzle loaf cake became a British home-baking standard in the late 20th century, popularised through cookbooks and baking shows. The technique of drizzling a sugar-and-lemon syrup over a still-warm pierced cake is specific to this recipe and produces the characteristic crunchy top crust. Earlier versions of lemon cake appear in British recipe books from the 19th century, but the drizzle technique is 20th century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the plain cake without the drizzle. Add the drizzle after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 3 to 4 days at room temperature. The texture improves on day 2.

content.text: The drizzle goes on while the cake is still warm and freshly out of the oven. Pierce the top all over with a skewer first so the syrup can reach the crumb rather than pooling on the surface. Use granulated sugar rather than caster for the drizzle: the coarser crystals are what creates the crunchy top.

Heat the oven to 180°C. Grease and line a 2 lb loaf tin. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar with a hand mixer until pale and fluffy. Add {{eggs}} eggs one at a time, beating well. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}} of flour, the zest of {{lemon}} lemons and {{whole-milk}} of milk. Pour into the prepared tin and bake for 40 to 45 minutes until risen, golden and a skewer comes out clean.

As soon as the cake comes from the oven, pierce the top all over with a skewer. Mix the juice of {{lemon}} lemons with {{granulated-sugar}} of granulated sugar and spoon slowly over the warm cake. Leave in the tin for 15 minutes until the drizzle sets, then remove and cool on a rack.

items.cause: Drizzle applied too late, or cake not pierced.
items.fix: Pierce all over while the cake is still hot and in the tin. Apply the drizzle immediately. The crust forms as the sugar cools on the warm surface.
Cream butter and sugar for 4 to 5 minutes. Fold the flour in quickly and briefly.
Check at 40 minutes. A well-drenched cake stays moist for 3 days. Don't skimp on the drizzle.

content.text: Lemon drizzle cake is one of the most consistently requested home bakes in Britain, appearing at every cake sale, coffee morning and bake-off. It requires almost nothing exotic and the result is reliably crowd-pleasing. The combination of sharp lemon and sweet crunchy crust makes it more interesting than the Victoria sponge it often sits beside on the same table.
